name: Philippines
overview: The Philippines' Data Privacy Act of 2012 (RA 10173) is a comprehensive law aligned with international standards, enforced by an active National Privacy Commission (NPC). The 2022 Implementing Rules refresh and a run of NPC circulars (breach management, consent, administrative fines, CCTV, digital lending) keep the regime current; amendments to raise penalties are periodically proposed in Congress.

legal_instruments.title: Data Privacy Act of 2012 (Republic Act No. 10173)
legal_instruments.summary: Comprehensive law establishing processing criteria, sensitive personal information rules, data subject rights, registration and DPO requirements, breach notification, and criminal penalties including imprisonment.

legal_instruments.title: Implementing Rules and Regulations of the Data Privacy Act
legal_instruments.summary: IRR detailing lawful processing, security measures, breach notification (72 hours to NPC and data subjects), outsourcing and accountability requirements.

legal_instruments.title: NPC Circular 2022-01 — Guidelines on Administrative Fines
legal_instruments.summary: Empowers the NPC to impose fines of 0.25%-3% of annual gross income for grave infractions and 0.25%-2% for major infractions, capped at PHP 5 million per infraction.

developments.title: NPC Circular on Consent (2023-04) enforcement begins
developments.summary: NPC guidance tightening validity requirements for consent-based processing took full effect, pushing controllers toward other lawful bases where appropriate.

developments.title: DPA amendment bills advance in Congress
developments.summary: Proposals to raise penalties, strengthen NPC enforcement powers and align the DPA with newer international norms continued through committee in the 19th/20th Congress.

penalties: Criminal: 1-6 years imprisonment plus fines up to PHP 5 million depending on the offence. Administrative: NPC fines up to 3% of annual gross income per infraction (capped PHP 5 million).
cross_border: Accountability model: the controller remains responsible for data transferred abroad and must use contractual or other reasonable means to ensure comparable protection.
